title: Works on My Machine
intro: It's Friday afternoon. You deploy a feature that passed all local testing. Five minutes later, the app crashes in production. Your console shows an error you've never seen before. Time to debug the most famous phrase in software: 'But it works on my machine!'

teach.title: The Kitchen Analogy
teach.body: Imagine a recipe that works perfectly in your kitchen but fails at a restaurant. Why? Different oven temperature, different altitude, different brand of flour. Your kitchen (development) and the restaurant (production) look similar but have subtle differences. The same happens with code -- your laptop and the server are different **environments** with different settings.

teach.title: The Top 5 'Works on My Machine' Bugs
teach.body:
These are the most common reasons code works locally but fails in production:

1. **Missing environment variables** -- You set them locally but forgot to add them on the server
2. **Case-sensitive file paths** -- Mac/Windows ignore case, Linux doesn't
3. **Different Node.js versions** -- Your laptop has v20, the server has v18
4. **Missing dependencies** -- A package is installed globally on your machine but not in package.json
5. **Hardcoded localhost URLs** -- localhost doesn't exist on the server

teach.title: A Sneaky Bug
teach.annotation: Each of these looks correct on your machine but will crash on the server for different reasons.

teach.body: 'Works on my machine' bugs are caused by differences between your local environment and the server. Always test your build locally, check env vars, and match Node versions.

start.narrative: You deployed a new feature at 4pm on Friday. The deployment succeeded, but users are reporting a blank white page. Your local version works perfectly. The production error log shows: 'TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading "NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL")'. Where do you start?

choices.text: Check if NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL is set in the hosting platform's environment variables
Roll back to the previous version immediately, investigate Monday
Add a try-catch around the code that uses that variable

check-env.narrative: You open the Vercel dashboard and look at the environment variables. NEXT_PUBLIC_API_URL is missing! You had it in your local .env file but never added it to Vercel. You add it and redeploy. But the site still shows a blank page. The logs now show a different error: 'Error: connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:5432'. What's happening?

choices.text: The DATABASE_URL is pointing to localhost -- it needs the production database URL
The database server is down -- contact the hosting provider

rollback-first.narrative: Rolling back gets the old version live again -- good instinct for protecting users. But you still need to fix the issue. Looking at the error message, it's about a missing environment variable. This is a configuration problem, not a code problem.

choices.text: Check the environment variables on the hosting platform

band-aid.narrative: A try-catch would silence the error but the feature would still be broken -- it needs that API URL to function. The root cause is that the variable doesn't exist in production. You need to set it up properly.

choices.text: Check the environment variables on the hosting platform

fix-db.narrative: You found it -- DATABASE_URL was set to the localhost connection string from development. You update it to the production database URL and redeploy. The site comes back to life! What's your final step to prevent this from happening again?

choices.text: Create a .env.example file listing all required variables and add a deploy checklist
Send a message to the team explaining what happened

wrong-diagnosis.narrative: 127.0.0.1 IS localhost -- the error is saying the app is trying to connect to a database on the same machine. But in production, the database is on a completely different server. The DATABASE_URL environment variable must be pointing to localhost.

choices.text: Update DATABASE_URL to the production database URL

restart.narrative: Restarting won't help -- the environment variable is still pointing to the wrong place. The error 'ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1' means the app is trying to connect to localhost, which doesn't have a database in production.

choices.text: Update DATABASE_URL to the production database URL

end-good.narrative: You create a .env.example file listing every required variable with placeholder values, add it to the repo, and update the team's deploy checklist to include 'verify all env vars are set'. Future deployments will be much safer.
end-good.feedback: Excellent debugging and prevention! You identified two missing environment variables, fixed them, and set up safeguards for the future. The .env.example pattern is industry standard -- it documents which variables your app needs without exposing the actual values.

end-okay.narrative: Good communication, but without a .env.example file or checklist, the same mistake will likely happen again the next time someone deploys. A systemic fix is better than a one-time message.
end-okay.feedback: You fixed the immediate issue -- nice work. But to prevent it from happening again, create a .env.example file that lists all required variables. This way, anyone deploying can quickly check that all variables are configured.
